1: A theme: the nations left are enumerated — the five lords of the Philistines, the Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites. Their persistent presence is YHWH’s deliberate provision of a testing environment for generations of Israelites who had not experienced the Exodus wars. The NT presents believers similarly as resident aliens in a world where opposition remains (1 Peter 2:11-12) — the testing environment is the school of covenant maturity.
2: A theme: the nations are left so that “the generations of the people of Israel might know war, to teach it to those who had not known it before.” Spiritual combat requires learning; the faith that overcomes the world (1 John 5:4) is formed through engagement with opposition. Christ himself learned obedience through what he suffered (Hebrews 5:8), and the pattern of growth through struggle is central to the NT discipleship vision.
3: A theme: the five Philistine lords — Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron — plus the Canaanites and Sidonians and Hivites remain. The identification of specific adversaries matters: they are not abstract evil but concrete historical opponents with territories and rulers. The NT is equally concrete about spiritual adversaries (Ephesians 6:12) — the Christian’s battle is not against vague forces but against specific principalities that have real hold in the world.
4: A theme: their purpose is stated explicitly — “to test Israel, to know whether Israel would obey the commandments of the LORD.” The test reveals what is already present but hidden: obedience or disobedience, faith or faithlessness. The testing by adversaries in the NT similarly reveals the quality of faith (1 Peter 1:7: “the tested genuineness of your faith, more precious than gold”), and Christ himself was tested to demonstrate the quality of his obedience (Matthew 4:1-11).
5: A shadow: Israel lives among the Canaanites — the reversal of the intended order. Instead of driving them out, Israel is surrounded. The pastoral situation assumes co-habitation with those whose practices are incompatible with covenant life. The NT calls for a similar navigating of presence-within-the-world-without-conformity-to-it (John 17:15: “not that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one”).
6: A shadow: intermarriage with the Canaanites and service to their gods — “they took their daughters as wives for themselves, and gave their own daughters to their sons, and served their gods.” The covenant purity of Israel is compromised by the most intimate human bond. The NT warns against the same: “do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers” (2 Corinthians 6:14), because the intimacy of partnership shapes the direction of worship.
7: A theme: “they forgot the LORD their God and served the Baals and the Asheroth.” Forgetting is the spiritual disease that idolatry produces: active replacement of YHWH with substitutes. The antidote in both Testaments is remembrance — the Passover, the Lord’s Supper, the daily recitation of the Shema. Christ establishes the Supper precisely as an act of remembrance against spiritual amnesia: “do this in remembrance of me” (Luke 22:19).
8: A theme: YHWH’s anger is “kindled” (ḥārāh) and he “sold” them to Cushan-rishathaim eight years. Divine anger as the covenantal consequence of apostasy is part of the judge cycle’s grammar; YHWH’s involvement in the oppression is not abandonment but active discipline. The eight-year span is the shortest oppression period in Judges — but even eight years of servitude is severe. The urgency of the deliverer needed is proportional to the severity of the bondage.
9: A type: “when the people of Israel cried out to the LORD, the LORD raised up a deliverer for the people of Israel, who saved them — Othniel the son of Kenaz.” This is the deliverer-type in its purest form: the cry of need, the divine response, the raising up of a specific person. The cry (zaʿaq) is a technical term for distress-petition; YHWH hears and acts by raising up (qûm, causing to stand up) his appointed deliverer. The NT’s declaration that God “raised up” Christ from the dead (Acts 2:32) uses the same conceptual framework — the ultimate deliverer raised up in response to the human need for redemption from death.
10: A type: “the Spirit of the LORD was upon him, and he judged Israel. He went out to war, and the LORD gave Cushan-rishathaim into his hand.” Othniel is the cleanest judge-type: Spirit-anointed, legally judging, militarily victorious — all by YHWH’s enabling. Each element maps directly onto Christ: the Spirit descends upon him at his baptism (Matthew 3:16); he judges with divine authority (John 5:22); he conquers the enemy through his death and resurrection. Isaiah 61:1, cited by Jesus in the Nazareth synagogue (Luke 4:18), describes the Spirit-anointed deliverer that Othniel anticipates.
11: A shadow: “the land had rest forty years.” The forty-year Sabbath-rest under Othniel evokes the wilderness generation (40 years) and Moses’ generational spans; forty is the biblical number of completion and testing. The rest is real but temporary — it ends with Othniel’s death. The Hebrews commentary on the Sabbath rest (Hebrews 4:1-11) is clear: the rest that Joshua and the judges provided was not the final rest; “there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God” — found only in Christ.
12: A theme: “the people of Israel again did what was evil.” The word “again” (wayyôsipû, they added/continued) marks the cycle’s resumption after the rest. Each resumption of the cycle deepens the reader’s sense of the spiral — Israel cannot sustain covenant faithfulness through their own will. The escalating apostasy documents the need for a regenerating work that changes the will itself (Ezekiel 36:26-27; John 3:3-8).
13: A theme: YHWH strengthens Eglon of Moab, who recruits Ammonites and Amalekites and defeats Israel, taking the city of palms (Jericho). The divinely strengthened enemy is the instrument of covenant discipline. The city of palms — associated with the conquest’s greatest victory — falls again into enemy hands, demonstrating that no past victory secures present faithfulness. The NT equivalent: past conversion does not guarantee ongoing discipleship; “work out your own salvation with fear and trembling” (Philippians 2:12).
14: A theme: eighteen years of servitude to Moab. The extended oppression (longer than the eight years under Cushan) signals that the second cycle is worse. The escalation of oppression throughout Judges follows the escalation of apostasy, pointing toward the need for a deliverer who does not merely alleviate but permanently ends the cycle.
15: A type: Israel cries out again, and YHWH raises up Ehud the son of Gera — “a left-handed man” (literally “a man bound/restricted in his right hand,” ʾitter yad-yəmînô). God’s deliberate choice of the weaker vessel (the left-handed was a disadvantage in ancient combat) anticipates the Pauline theology of divine power through human weakness: “God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong” (1 Corinthians 1:27). The deliverer is unexpected; so is Christ — “can anything good come out of Nazareth?” (John 1:46).
16: A shadow: Ehud’s double-edged sword, hidden on his right thigh and drawn with his left hand, is the instrument of deliverance. The double-edged sword as an image for the Word of God — “the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword” (Hebrews 4:12) — gives the weapon symbolic weight. Ehud’s unexpected instrument recalls the divine pattern of using what appears weak or unsuitable to accomplish what no conventional weapon could.
17: A theme: the presentation of tribute to Eglon — “a very fat man” — establishes the scene. The gross physicality of the oppressor (his obesity becomes the detail that makes the assassination possible in v22) is a narrative device that makes him humanly contemptible and the deliverance humanly satisfying. The narrator is not squeamish; the reality of oppressive power and its physical embodiment are honestly portrayed.
18: A theme: Ehud sends the tribute-bearers away after the presentation. He acts alone in the assassination — no witnesses, no army. The pattern of the solitary deliverer acting in secret recurs throughout Scripture: Gideon reducing his army (Judges 7), Samson alone against the Philistines. Christ enters the decisive battle of Gethsemane and the cross alone; “I looked, but there was no one to help” (Isaiah 63:5).
19: A shadow: “I have a secret message for you, O king.” The Hebrew daḇar-sēter (secret word/thing) carries double meaning: a word of God that is also a physical “word” (the sword). The divine word comes as revelation but also as judgment. The NT presents Christ as the Word of God (John 1:1) whose coming is both revelation and judgment: “for judgment I came into this world” (John 9:39).
20: A shadow: “I have a message from God for you” — Ehud’s words are literally true; he is YHWH’s instrument carrying YHWH’s judgment. Eglon rises to receive the “word of God” — and receives a literal blade. The irony is sharp: the oppressor who made Israel his subjects rises in respect for the divine word, only to receive it as judgment. The scene anticipates the judgment that comes on all who honor God merely with their lips (Isaiah 29:13; Matthew 15:8) while continuing in opposition to his purposes.
21: A theme: the sudden execution — Ehud’s left hand draws the blade from his right thigh and thrusts it into Eglon’s belly. The reversal of handedness (left-handed warrior drawing from the right side) is the surprise that makes the assassination possible. Divine deliverance regularly works through the reversal of expectations: the younger over the elder (Jacob), the small over the great (David and Goliath), the weak over the mighty (Christ’s cross defeating death).
22: A theme: the sword sinks in completely — “the dirt came out.” The completeness of the fatal blow is emphasized: nothing is left undone. Christ’s declaration “it is finished” (tetelestai, John 19:30) is the NT equivalent — the work of redemption is complete, nothing partial, nothing requiring supplementation.
23: A theme: Ehud escapes through the misdərôn (vestibule/porch) and locks the doors — creating the delay that enables his escape (Judges 3:24-26). The divinely timed exit from the site of judgment is a recurring motif: Lot escaping Sodom (Gen 19), Israel crossing the Red Sea, and ultimately Christ’s resurrection — escaping death and leaving the tomb behind.
24: A theme: the servants assume Eglon is relieving himself in the cool chamber — a moment of dark comedy. Their assumption delays the discovery and enables Ehud’s escape. The enemies of God’s purposes are confused about what has happened; the divine plan proceeds while they wait in bewilderment. This pattern of enemy confusion enabling deliverance appears in the Easter narrative: “they sealed the tomb” (Matthew 27:66) thinking the story was over.
25: A theme: the servants wait until they are embarrassed, then take the key and find Eglon dead. The discovery comes too late to prevent the deliverer’s escape. The enemies’ slowness to understand what has happened allows the work of God to reach completion before opposition can organize. The NT parallel: those who sought to stop the resurrection by sealing and guarding the tomb found it empty — too late to prevent what had already been accomplished.
26: A theme: while they delayed, Ehud escaped past the stone idols at Gilgal and reached safety. The idols at Gilgal — the site of Israel’s covenant renewal after the Jordan crossing — stand as a sad commentary on the spiritual state of the land. The deliverer passes the corrupted sacred site on his way to freedom, representing the triumph of covenant fidelity over compromised religion.
27: A type: Ehud sounds the trumpet in the hill country of Ephraim, and the Israelites follow him into battle. The deliverer who accomplished the decisive blow now calls the community to participate in the victory. The pattern is identical to the resurrection and Pentecost sequence: Christ accomplishes the decisive victory, then the Spirit-sent proclamation (the “trumpet” of the gospel, 1 Corinthians 15:52; Revelation 11:15) calls the community to follow him into the ongoing battle.
28: A type: “Follow me, for the LORD has given your enemies the Moabites into your hand.” Ehud’s battle cry is the deliverer’s call to discipleship in the moment of victory. Jesus’s “follow me” calls are the NT expression of the same: the deliverer who has already won the decisive battle invites his people to join in the ongoing conquest (Matthew 4:19; Judges 16:24). The victory is certain because YHWH has already “given” the enemy.
29: A theme: ten thousand strong, able-bodied Moabites killed — “not a man escaped.” The completeness of the victory mirrors the promised total defeat of all enemies in the eschatological battle (Revelation 19:11-21). The number ten thousand (a myriad) is the biblical superlative for a vast army; their complete defeat signals the thoroughness of YHWH’s deliverance.
30: A shadow: “Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel. And the land had rest for eighty years.” The longest rest in Judges (80 years, a double generation) follows the Ehud deliverance. The extended rest is the closest the book comes to the permanent Sabbath-rest of the eschatological kingdom. But even 80 years ends; only the rest in Christ — the true Sabbath-rest that Hebrews 4:9 names — is permanent and unending.
31: A shadow: Shamgar kills six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad — an agricultural implement repurposed as a weapon. He also “saved Israel.” The pattern of extraordinary deliverance through unexpected means (an oxgoad; cf. Samson with a jawbone of a donkey, Judges 15) anticipates the cross: the instrument of shame and death repurposed as the instrument of salvation. Paul calls the preaching of the cross “folly” to those who are perishing but “the power of God” to those being saved (1 Corinthians 1:18). God consistently chooses the instrument that human wisdom would reject.
